title: Quiz: CI/CD for Quantum Applications
description: Test your understanding of continuous integration and deployment practices for quantum software projects

questions.question: What is the primary challenge that distinguishes quantum CI/CD from classical CI/CD?

options.text: Quantum code is written in different programming languages
Quantum outputs are probabilistic and require statistical validation rather than exact comparisons
Quantum computers are faster than classical computers
Quantum code cannot be version controlled

questions.explanation: Unlike classical software where tests produce deterministic results, quantum computations produce probabilistic outcomes. This requires statistical validation techniques like chi-squared tests and confidence intervals rather than simple equality checks.

questions.question: In a quantum CI/CD pipeline, why should hardware validation tests typically run less frequently than simulator tests?

options.text: Hardware tests are less accurate than simulator tests
Hardware tests have costs, queue times, and limited quotas compared to local simulators
Simulators provide better fidelity than real hardware
Hardware is only available during business hours

questions.explanation: Quantum hardware access involves costs (usage quotas), significant queue times, and limited availability. Running hardware tests on every commit would be expensive and slow. Instead, hardware tests are typically scheduled periodically or triggered manually for release candidates.

questions.question: Which testing strategy is most appropriate for validating that a Bell state preparation circuit works correctly?

options.text: Check that the circuit has exactly 2 gates
Verify the statevector and use chi-squared test on measurement distributions
Run the circuit once and check if the output is |00> or |11>
Compare the QASM output to a reference string

questions.explanation: Proper validation involves verifying the exact statevector matches the expected Bell state (1/sqrt(2))(|00> + |11>) and using statistical tests on measurement outcomes to confirm the 50-50 distribution between |00> and |11> with high confidence.

questions.question: What is the purpose of noise model simulation in a quantum CI/CD pipeline?

options.text: To make simulators run faster
To test how circuits perform under realistic hardware-like conditions before using actual hardware
To add artificial errors to passing tests
To reduce the number of test cases needed

questions.explanation: Noise model simulation bridges the gap between ideal simulation and hardware execution. It allows testing circuit resilience to realistic errors (depolarizing noise, readout errors) without incurring hardware costs, catching issues before expensive hardware validation.

questions.question: How should quantum hardware credentials (API tokens) be handled in CI/CD pipelines?

options.text: Store them in the repository's .env file
Use encrypted secrets and environment protection rules, never store in code
Include them in the CI/CD configuration file for easy access
Share them in the team's documentation wiki

questions.explanation: Quantum hardware tokens must be stored as encrypted secrets in the CI/CD platform (e.g., GitHub Secrets) and accessed via environment variables. Environment protection rules can require approvals before accessing production credentials.

questions.question: What metric would be most important to monitor during a canary deployment of a new quantum circuit version?

options.text: The color scheme of the quantum circuit visualization
Execution fidelity, success rate, and error rates compared to the stable version
The number of lines of code in the new version
The time it takes to write documentation

questions.explanation: Canary deployments for quantum services should monitor quantum-specific metrics like output fidelity and success rates. These determine whether the new circuit version performs at least as well as the current production version under real traffic conditions.

questions.question: Which of the following should be automated in a quantum CI/CD pipeline? (Select all that apply)

options.text: Circuit transpilation and optimization for target backends
Statistical validation of measurement distributions
Circuit depth and gate count checks against thresholds
Selection of which quantum algorithm to use for each problem

questions.explanation: Circuit optimization, statistical validation, and metric checks should all be automated in the pipeline. Algorithm selection is a design decision made by developers, not an automated pipeline step.

questions.question: What is the recommended approach for testing layers in a quantum CI/CD pipeline?

options.text: Run all tests on hardware to ensure maximum accuracy
Progress through layers: classical tests, statevector, noise simulation, then hardware
Only test on simulators since hardware is unreliable
Skip simulation and go directly to hardware for faster feedback

questions.explanation: A layered approach catches issues early and cheaply. Classical tests run first, then ideal statevector simulation, then noise model simulation, and finally hardware validation. Each layer adds confidence while controlling costs and feedback time.
